3: Poultry sector, the fastest expanding livestock sector, is the leader of the global meat production growth in response to rising demand for this healthy high protein and low fat type of meat (FAO, 2013; OECD-FAO,2016). Poultry is the most eaten meat after pork in the world (14.99 and kg/capita/year, respectively) and is ex- pected to takeover the leading position in the future (FAOSTAT, 2017; OECD-FAO, 2015). Low-priced product and production have broadened network of poultry producers and consumers, which caused estimation that chicken meat chains will emit 0.6 giga- tonnes CO2-equivalent and that minimization of emission intensity is mandatory (MacLeod et al., 2013; OECD-FAO, 2016).
Common environmental performance indicators in the poultry sector are water consumption, energy consumption, feed

production, usage of chemicals and packaging materials, waste water discharge and waste treatment (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; González-García et al., 2014). All indicators are calculated per 1 kg of product as outlined in Food and Agriculture Organiza- tion of the United Nations report (MacLeod et al., 2013) and in line with other life cycle assessment (LCA) studies related to chicken/ poultry meat (Cesari et al., 2017; Grandl et al., 2012).

Life cycle assessment is the collection and interpretation of the inputs, outputs and possible environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle and a tool for the analysis of the environmental load of products at all stages in their life cycle (ISO, 2006). It indicates hotspots in the production chain, while giving the opportunity for lowering environmental burden and improving efficiency and profitability (Eide, 2002; Fiala, 2008).

There are papers analyzing environmental impact of the poultry farm and slaughterhouse (Cesari et al., 2017; da Silva et al., 2014; González-García et al., 2014; Kalhor et al., 2016), but there is a lack of environmental research concerning life cycle assessment approach for an overall chicken meat chain (Skunca et al., 2015).

Table 1 gives an overview of the LCA studies in poultry meat sector, emphasizing hotspots, system boundaries and environmental im- pacts. The predominant environmental indicators related to the chicken meat chain were Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidi- fication Potential (AP), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Cumulative Energy Demand (CED), as well as Ozone Layer Depletion (OLD).

The meat production sector is one of the dominant food sectors in Serbia (Djekic et al., 2015). According to official data of Statistical Office of the Republic of Serbia for 2016 (SORS, 2017) it can be considered that annual production of live chicken in Serbia was 9.31 X 107 kg, while outcome from slaughterhouses is 6.88 X 107 kg, out of which 1.72 X 107 kg are processed in meat processing plants.

There are only two studies on environmental impacts of an overall poultry meat chain (Pardo et al., 2012; Weidema et al., 2008). Weidema's research represents the systematic overview on the life cycle of poultry meat in the European Union countries and not the case study, while Pardo's study is concentrated on potential improvements and not on in-depth LCA analysis of all 5 subsystems. The novelty lies in the fact that to the best of our knowledge and available data this research presents the first life cycle assessment of entire chicken meat chain in this part of Europe. The significance of this study is reproducibility of the methodology to other regions within the global poultry industry.

The objective of this study was to analyze the environmental performance of 119 different farms, slaughterhouses, meat pro- cessors and retailers, as well as 500 households from a cradle-to- grave perspective. A total of 619 life cycle assessment calculations have been performed.

The predominant environmental indicators related to the chicken meat chain were Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidi- fication Potential (AP), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Cumulative Energy Demand (CED), as well as Ozone Layer Depletion (OLD).

The meat production sector is one of the dominant food sectors in Serbia (Djekic et al., 2015). According to official data of Statistical Office of the Republic of Serbia for 2016 (SORS, 2017) it can be considered that annual production of live chicken in Serbia was 9.31 X 107 kg, while outcome from slaughterhouses is 6.88 X 107 kg, out of which 1.72 X 107 kg are processed in meat processing plants.

Weidema's research represents the systematic overview on the life cycle of poultry meat in the European Union countries and not the case study, while Pardo's study is concentrated on potential improvements and not on in-depth LCA analysis of all 5 subsystems. The novelty lies in the fact that to the best of our knowledge and available data this research presents the first life cycle assessment of entire chicken meat chain in this part of Europe. 3: LCA framework applied in this research was established ac- cording to the standard published by the International Organiza- tion for Standardization (ISO). It combined the consecutive steps: mapping the process (defining the objective), setting scope and boundaries, collecting data, calculating and analyzing the results (ISO, 2006).
4: After the goal of the research has been determined, the chosen functional unit (FU), which confers a reference to which inputs and outputs are referred to, was established (Djekic et al., 2014) as 1 kg of consumed chicken meat and chicken meat products (1.35 kg of live weight chicken is transformed to functional unit according to good manufacturing process in Serbia). Inputs for the LCA calcu- lations included feed for broilers, which consists of maize, soybean and wheat, fresh water, electricity, fossil fuels, packaging materials and cleaning agents, while outputs covered plastic and paper waste, biowaste and waste water. Energy consumption examined in this study included electrical and thermal energy used for farm, slaughterhouse and processing plant activities, as well as its usage in the retail stores and households connected to chicken meat and chicken meat products. Activities taken into consideration were live animals transportation from farm to the slaughterhouse, trans- portation of carcasses from slaughterhouse to the processing plant, transportation of chicken meat and chicken meat products from plant to the retail store and all chicken meat and chicken meat products related actions within farm, slaughterhouse, meat pro- cessing plant, retail and household.
5: This research aims to evaluate the environmental impacts throughout the life cycle of chicken meat chain through a cradle-to-

grave perspective and to propose improvement actions for the environmental burdens reduction.
The system boundaries covered five subsystems 'chicken farm', 'slaughterhouse', 'meat processing plant, 'retail' and 'household use'.
Subsystem 1 - chicken farm: chicken housing farm covered all activities which take place in a farm related to the raising broilers, such as feeding, watering, cleaning and waste handling.

There were 20 chicken farms included in this study. According to their total annual output, these farms represent over 67% of total broilers produced annually in poultry industry in the Republic of Serbia (SORS, 2017).

Subsystem 2 - slaughterhouse: this subsystem included all ac- tivities which take place in a slaughterhouse, from reception of live chickens, covering livestock handling and animal welfare, slaugh- tering (stunning, bleeding, scalding and defeathering, evisceration, removing of head and feet) and chilling and freezing (Verheijen et al., 1996). Waste handling, cleaning and transport of live chickens from farm to the slaughterhouse were also used in the calculations.
Nine slaughterhouses were included in this research. According to their total annual output, these slaughterhouses represent over
77% of total broiler carcasses produced annually in poultry industry in the Republic of Serbia (SORS, 2017).

Subsystem 3 - meat processing plant: this subsystem included all activities which take place in a processing plant, from reception of carcasses, covering preparation activities, thermal processing, packaging, storage of final products and waste handling. Cleaning and carcass transportation from slaughterhouse to meat processing plant was used in the calculations.

Eight meat processing plants were included in this study. Ac- cording to their total annual output, these plants represent over 60% of estimated total annually processed chicken meat in poultry industry in the Republic of Serbia.

Subsystem 4 - retail: this subsystem included all chicken meat and chicken meat products related activities which take place in a retail store, including storage, chilling, use of cleaning agents and waste handling.
Transport of processed chicken meat and chicken meat products from meat processing plant to retail was used in the calculations.
This study included 82 retail stores that sell over 50% of total chicken meat and chicken meat products estimated to sell annually in poultry industry in the Republic of Serbia.

19: Life cycle impact assessment stage of the standardized LCA methodology implied classification and characterization stages that were undertaken in this study (ISO, 2006). Environmental impact potentials calculated in this research were global warming poten- tial, cumulative energy demand, ozone layer depletion, acidifica- tion potential and eutrophication potential. Average values and standard deviation range of the GWP, CED, OLD and AP per func- tional unit are shown in Figs. 3-6. Relative contributions of pro- cesses involved in all subsystems are shown in Fig. 7.
20: For GWP, CED, AP and EP farm dominates with the impact on the environment, due to feed and energy. On the other hand, mixed plastics and paper waste treatments were slightly lowering envi- ronmental impact as a result of recycling. In our research subsystem 1 had the same average score of 1.81 kg CO2 eq as the other 4 sub- systems together. GWP results vary in observed farms from 1.71 to 2.36 kg CO2 eq, which is a result of energy usage. These results are in accordance with other LCA studies including broiler chicken (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; González-García et al., 2014). For subsystem 1 CED results were between 10.1 and 22.6 MJ, which is in line with scores presented in other research (Cesari et al., 2017; Grandl et al., 2012), but lower than in Baumgartner's study (Baumgartner et al., 2008). For farms OLD results were between

77.7 and 238 ug CFC-11 eq and feed and energy had the highest influence. These results were much higher than in Pelletier's research (Pelletier, 2008), but significantly lower than in Kalhor's study (Kalhor et al., 2016). AP results were between 52.9 and 68.4 SO2 eq. Similar results were presented in Baumgartner's research (Baumgartner et al., 2008). AP for farms is higher than in Cesari's, da Silva's, Pelletier's and Kalhor's research (Cesari et al., 2017; da Silva et al., 2014; Kalhor et al., 2016; Pelletier, 2008). EP results were very similar among farms, ranging between 1.39 and 1.43 g PO4 eq. with an average of 1.4g PO4 eq. These results are lower than those pre- sented in most of the studies (da Silva et al., 2014; Kalhor et al., 2016).

A farm with a market share of 3.02% was energy inefficient by using 5.38 MJ for production, which was more than 7 times higher than the average (Table 2). As a result this farm had the highest values for GWP, CED, OLD and AP in comparison to other farms. A farm with a 2.69% market share had the lowest values for GWP and CED as a result of lower energy usage.

On the other hand, mixed plastics and paper waste treatments were slightly lowering envi- ronmental impact as a result of recycling. 77.7 and 238 ug CFC-11 eq and feed and energy had the highest influence. AP results for slaughterhouses vary vastly among the different authors (Table 1).

30: The results obtained in this research are in line with other LCA studies in which different chicken meat chain subsystems are analyzed. In the researches where farm was included (Table 1), the farm activities expressed highest environmental impacts through GWP, AP, EP and CED indicators. Crucial environmental hotspot for environmental impact categories is production of feed, but the impacts of other stages in the life cycle of the entire chicken meat chain are also vital to examine.

Contributions of four processes involved in the chicken meat chain to GWP have been examined, since activities, inputs and outputs are influenced by them (Fig. 7). The first process, 'Feed' is part of subsystem 1 and consists of maize, soybean and wheat production as feed ingredients. All types of energy and water used across the entire chain were grouped under second process 'Re- sources'. Third process named 'Waste&waste water' considered all types of waste and waste water from all subsystems. The fourth process 'Other' included all packaging materials and cleaning agents used in subsystems 1-4, as well as transport between first 4 subsystems.

'Feed' was identified as the largest contributor to GWP in first subsystem (93.7%) (Fig. 7). Environmental burden of feed produc- tion and on-farm emissions emphasis the importance of clean renewable energy usage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014; González-García et al., 2014).
'Resources' were the prevailing factor for environmental

burdens for retail (87.5%) and household use (98.3%). Also, 'Re- sources' had predominant contributions for slaughterhouse (62.7%) followed by 'Waste&waste water' (35.8%) (mainly biowaste), as well as for processing plant (79.9%), followed by 'Other' (19.9%) (mainly packaging materials). Surveyed farms and slaughterhouses use electricity and fossil fuels, particularly diesel fuels, liquid petrol gas (LPG) and natural gas, while households also reported usage of wood chips. Processing plants and retail stores use electricity. 'Re- sources' emissions due to fossil fuels usage were lightly contrib- uting to GWP while electricity contributed in a much higher extent. By using alternative fuels 70% of greenhouse gas emissions from broiler houses could be cut, although it would result in a 7% in- crease in tropospheric ozone formation because of increased air emissions (Katajajuuri et al., 2008).

It is important to note that farms and slaughterhouses included in the study were in the same region of Serbia, while processing plants were located in close proximity to the slaughterhouses and retail stores so transport had a minor influence on the chicken meat chain environmental impacts.

33: Similar to the Tassielli's and Groen's study a sensitivity analysis was carried out by changing the assessment method from IMPACT 2002+ (V2.12) to CML IA baseline (V3.03) (Groen et al., 2016; Tassielli et al., 2018) and distinguishing between the influence and the importance of certain input parameters on assessment method change. The influence and importance of parameters 'Feed', 'Elec- tric energy', "Transport" and 'Water' is shown in Fig. 8. The hori- zontal axis ranks the most influential parameters, while the vertical axis ranks the most important parameters. Reduction of influential parameters will directly reduce the environmental impacts. On the other side, important parameters reflect output uncertainty. Essential parameter is both influential and important, while minor parameter has low influence and importance (Fig. 8). This type of analysis identifies parameters that can be used to identify potential mitigation strategies.

Method change had the highest influence on essential param- eter 'Feed', since GWP was 12.39% higher, while OLD remained the same. 'Electric energy' was important parameter, as GWP was 3.15-4.0% higher and OLD was 5.62-5.92% lower. Influential parameter "Transport' had the same OLD, but GWP was 1.31-1.82% higher. For 'Water' as a minor parameter, both GWP and OLD remained almost the same.

Gonzales-Garcia's research results were used for farms and slaughterhouses air and water emissions, while Kalhor's study was used for COD and BOD5 water emissions (González-García et al., 2014; Kalhor et al., 2016). For farms results for OLD and CED remained the same, while GWP results were 1.64-2.92% higher. For slaughterhouses results for GWP, OLD and CED were unchanged (data not shown).

35: Key stakeholders in the chicken meat chain represented by farms, slaughterhouses and processing plants of substantial size are in control of production processes, while simultaneously taking active role in lowering environmental burden. On the other hand, smaller stakeholders have lower level of environmental protection as a result of financial deficiency. Possible improvement actions that can be undertaken in farms, slaughterhouses, processing plants, retail stores and households are depending on the financial resources and companies management willingness to implement changes. Improvements for reducing environmental contributions

over the entire life cycle of the chicken meat chain could be introduced in four directions. The first direction suggests grain le- gumes utilization in feed. Baumgartner reported that when grain legumes serve as a protein source in feed instead of soybean-based ingredients it decreases the environmental impact, since the cultivation of grain legumes doesn't require mineral fertilizer application (Baumgartner et al., 2008). Second direction is con- nected to treating of chicken litter in a biogas digester. Mainali reported that if the litter produced in the chicken farm is treated in an anaerobic digester, and produced biogas is used for generating electricity, then the greenhouse gases emission intensity will be reduced by 76%, and if biogas is used to replace LPG for cooking purposes, the emission will be reduced by 65% (Mainali et al., 2017). Third action relates to energy. Essentially, reducing fuel and elec- tricity use within the chicken meat life cycle has environmental and economic impact due to the reduction in emissions and cost- effectiveness (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013). Energy savings can be achieved through utilization of energy efficient systems in farms, slaughterhouses and meat processing plants, as well as energy efficient chilling equipment in retail. Utilization of energy efficient refrigerators, freezers and stoves in households should be encour- aged, as consumers will pay lower monthly energy bill, while at the same time downsizing their environmental footprint. Fourth di- rection is focused on mitigation options for environmental impacts optimization which rely on recycling of household waste. Con- sumers should be stimulated and encouraged to recycle their chicken meat and chicken meat products related waste in the aim of more sustainable consumption (Davis and Sonesson, 2008).

37: The chicken meat production is among the prevailing food sectors, as the poultry will lead world consumption of meat in the future. The study covered the life cycle assessment from a farm, slaughterhouse, meat processing plant, retail and household perspective and displayed, for the first time, environmental im- pacts of the entire chicken meat chain, while including 119 chicken production stakeholders and 500 households in the research.

This research confirmed previous studies that the main contributor to the environmental profile of a chicken farm is feed production. The activities performed at the farm express the highest environmental impacts manifested as GWP, CED, AP and EP. Contributions of the meat processing plant are essentially due to energy use and packaging materials, while impact of slaughter- house, retail and household is directly connected to energy requirement.

Research results are closely related to raw data acquired from company management and can be used as a basis for discussion in order to make environmental improvements. While most of the attained data are in line with the literature, there is still a possibility for minimization of the environmental impacts of poultry pro- duction in Serbia, limited by the degree to which the improvement option can be implemented.

The study conclusions are valuable to the chicken meat industry stakeholders and could encourage grain legumes usage as a protein source in feed, treating of chicken litter in a biogas digester, usage of energy efficient systems through the entire chain, while at the same time stimulating consumers towards more sustainable con- sumption in terms of waste recycling. Implementation of the same methodology in other countries could offer a better insight into environmental practices within the global poultry production.

While most of the attained data are in line with the literature, there is still a possibility for minimization of the environmental impacts of poultry pro- duction in Serbia, limited by the degree to which the improvement option can be implemented.

The study conclusions are valuable to the chicken meat industry stakeholders and could encourage grain legumes usage as a protein source in feed, treating of chicken litter in a biogas digester, usage of energy efficient systems through the entire chain, while at the same time stimulating consumers towards more sustainable con- sumption in terms of waste recycling. Implementation of the same methodology in other countries could offer a better insight into environmental practices within the global poultry production.
